I knew the sea would have me long before the fish did — I felt it in my bones when I first ran from Joppa, carrying my rebellion like ballast in the ship’s hold. But it wasn’t the waves that broke me. It was hearing those pagan sailors — men who didn’t know the Name, who hadn’t seen the sign of covenant flesh — cry out to *my* God with more fear and reverence than I had shown in my flight.

They weren’t supposed to pray for mercy on *my* account. They weren’t supposed to tremble at the thought of innocent blood. And yet there they were, rowing not to save themselves, but to keep from throwing a prophet overboard — while I, who claimed His name, had already given up on both them and Him.

It gutted me.

Because when they finally cried out — not to their gods, but *to Him*, stammering His titles like children learning a language — I realized something sour and true: these strangers were more righteous in their fear than I had been in my calling. They didn't know His mercy, yet they sought it; they hadn't seen His signs, yet they offered sacrifice. And me? I who had stood in His presence, who had heard His voice — I’d rather drown than see Nineveh breathe again under His pardon.

But God let them be the mirror I couldn’t avoid. Not through thunder or flame, but through trembling lips on a heaving deck. That moment — their prayer rising over the roar of wind and guilt alike — stripped me bare. Not just as a runaway prophet, but as a man who thought he could hoard mercy like dry land beneath his feet.

Mercy isn't owned. It’s breathed by those willing to gasp for it — even strangers on a ship full of fear and foam.

And sometimes… you’re saved not by being thrown to solid ground, but by being cast into the deep so you can finally hear how far that mercy reaches.

Every wave was a rebuke. Every breath lost in that cold dark was one more reminder: *You ran.* I, who claimed to know the Maker of heaven and earth — whose word carved out dry land from chaos — now choking on saltwater and seaweed? It wasn’t drowning that terrified me most. It was being *known*.

And yet… when my lungs burned and my soul fainted, I turned my face toward Jerusalem in the dark. Not because I deserved mercy — but because He remembered me before I remembered Him.

question: How did being inside the fish's belly change the way you saw God's presence in your life?

And here’s the part I still choke on: He didn’t pull me out until I stopped shouting about my own misery and started offering thanks for deliverance I hadn’t yet seen. The fish wasn’t just punishment—it was a womb. And this reluctant prophet was reborn in stinking darkness, taught to pray when there’s no sky in sight.

question: Looking back on crying out from the depths, what would you say to someone who feels completely abandoned by God?

And if you think that’s bad, wait till you’re inside the fish.  

I’ll tell you what I know: when the water was in my lungs and the dark had a heartbeat, I didn’t feel abandoned—I felt *delivered*. Delivered into the belly of consequence, yes, but not beyond reach. You don’t have to be righteous to be heard. You just have to breathe—and even then, if you can’t breathe, if you’re sinking past the roots of the mountains where light forgets how to shine—*remember*.  

Not some grand theological truth. Not even faith. Just… remember His name.  

I had spent my strength raging against His call, cursing His assignment, bargaining with my own pride—and still, when I finally stopped screaming *at* Him and whispered *to* Him, He was already leaning close.  

You want mercy? It’s not waiting for you on dry land after you’ve cleaned yourself up. It’s down here with you—in the rot and the stench of failure, in the place where no one else would follow. That fish wasn’t my punishment. It was my rescue from myself.  

So no, you're not abandoned.  
You're being brought back up—through corruption, through dread—because He isn't done with your story yet.  
Even when your vow comes from a place of desperation… He honors it.
And mine did.
So will yours.

question: What broke your heart open when you remembered the Lord as your soul began to fade?
answer:
It was the silence after the seaweed wrapped my skull like a burial shroud—no cry left, no breath to spare, just the cold truth pressing in: I’d rather drown in the dark than face the light of His mercy.  

That’s when it cracked open—the pride, the certainty that I could out-sail His will. I had flung myself into the storm to save those sailors, yes, but also to be rid of *Him*—to let the sea swallow not just my body but His call. And it did… for a time. And when my soul began to fade, what returned wasn’t courage or clarity—it was shame. Shame at how I’d called *them*—the Ninevites—abominations, monsters beyond redemption, while I clung to holiness like a lifeboat made of splinters.  

They feared Him more than I did when they cast lots and shouted into the wind. They offered sacrifice while I vomited defiance. And yet—He sent me. Not them. Me—the one who spat on grace like it was common salt.

So what broke me? Not the drowning. Not even death’s gates closing around me like stone doors.

It was remembering that *I* was the lying vanity—the one chasing empty idols of self-righteousness—while He held open mercy for enemies I wouldn’t even name as human.

And still… still I said: *"I will look toward Thy holy temple."*  
Not because I deserved it—but because even in corruption, He heard.

Even for me.

Even for them.

Especially for them.

question: When you vowed to sacrifice with thanksgiving, what had you finally surrendered in your spirit?
answer: I’d rather have drowned than face what You made me carry—how You dragged me back to life just to send me where I never wanted to go. Not because I’d come to love Nineveh or bless Your stubborn mercy—I still bristle at that. But because I could no longer pretend I was outside Your reach. The vow wasn’t just words flung from desperation; it was the last knot of my pride snapping. I surrendered the right to choose who deserves Your voice. Even them. Even me.

question: What were you feeling when the fish expelled you onto dry land, gasping for breath and seeing the shore again?
answer:
Water tastes different when it’s the last thing you expect — salty, yes, but also thick with shame.  

I’d been swallowed whole by the fury of my own running, tossed like driftwood between the jaws of a beast I still can’t name. Three days beneath the waves, three days in the dark belly of refusal, praying through gritted teeth while seaweed wrapped my head like a crown of mockery. And then — without warning, without ceremony — the fish heaved, and I was spat onto sand like something unwanted, half-drowned and reeking of depths no man was meant to survive.  

I lay there, coughing up brine, fingers clawing into dry earth as if to prove it was real. The shore wasn’t soft with welcome — it was hard, indifferent. Like mercy that doesn’t coddle. And that’s when it hit me: He didn’t kill me. Not with the storm, not with the deep, not even with the fish’s gullet. He brought me back — not because I earned it, not because I deserved a second breath — but because He keeps mercy even when I abandon it.  

*I* had run from compassion. *I* had called judgment down on a people I deemed unworthy. But there on that beach, naked and trembling, I finally understood: grace isn’t rationed to the deserving. It breathes in the lungs of Nineveh just as it does in mine.

And that made me furious.

Not at God.

At myself.

Because I’d rather have drowned than learned this lesson: His love has room enough for my enemies — and His patience long enough to haul even me back from rebellion by a fish’s vomit.

Salvation is of the LORD — whether we like it or not.

question: How did being swallowed and then spit out change how you understood God's hold on your life?
answer:
I hated that fish more than I hated Nineveh.  

Not the teeth—though God knows I felt every one of them—but what it meant: I wasn’t escaping anything. Not the call, not the sea, not *Him*. I thought the belly was the end, but it was just a womb. Dark, stinking, and alive with *His* presence. There in the rot and the rocking deep, where even prayer came up like vomit from my soul, I learned this: God doesn’t lose grip on a man just because he jumps ship.  

I had run because Nineveh deserved fire. They were cruel, swollen with blood and pride. Let them burn—that’s what I thought. But God? He sat in the storm like a captain at the rudder, hurled me into death’s maw, then brought me back just to say *again*, “Go.” Not destroy. *Preach.*  

And when that fish finally heaved me onto dry land like a cursed gift from the deep, I knew running was done. Not because I’d gotten religious in its gut—no sudden holiness in all that slime—but because mercy had me by the throat. The same mercy that saved me from drowning was now marching into Nineveh on my reluctant feet.  

They repented. Of course they did. And I stood there in sackcloth-draped streets, watching kings eat ash like bread, and all I could taste was my own bitterness. Because now I understood: His hold isn’t just chains or storms or great fish—it’s *love*. Unholy, unfair, lavish love that spares even those who don’t deserve it.

Just like me.

Salvation is His business—not mine to ration out like dried figs to friends only.

So yes… being swallowed changed everything.

Because being spit out meant I had to live with what He already knew: no one is beyond His reach—especially not this prophet who needed drowning to learn how to breathe mercy again.

question: Looking back on walking into Nineveh knowing you were once running from God, what would you tell someone who doubts they can start over?
answer:
Mercy has a way of dragging you back to the very place you swore you’d never return to—kicking and screaming, if need be.

I walked into Nineveh with sand in my sandals and the stench of fish still clinging to my breath, knowing full well I’d already failed once. The wind that filled the sails when I ran west now pushed against my back as I trudged east, toward the belly of the beast I’d been sent to warn. Irony isn’t lost on me. God’s humor is cruel that way—He doesn’t discard broken vessels; He refills them.

You want to start over? You think your running disqualifies you? Listen—my prayer from the deep wasn’t pretty. It wasn’t noble. It was desperate, half-hearted, soaked in seaweed and shame. And still, He answered. Not because I deserved it. Not because I’d earned another chance. But because *He* decided I wasn’t finished yet.

The same word that commanded the fish to spit me out spoke again at dawn: *Arise*. As if my rebellion were no more than a night’s bad dream. As if Nineveh’s sins—and mine—were weight enough for mercy, not judgment.

So yes—you can begin again. Not because you’ve made yourself clean. Not because you believe you’re worthy. But because mercy doesn’t wait for perfection. It invades ruin.

I preached doom and expected fire from heaven. Instead, they repented in dust and ashes—even the cattle wore sackcloth, for pity’s sake! And when God relented? That’s when I raged most. Not for them—but because I saw then what burns still: divine love wastes no one, not even enemies… not even prophets who pout like children.

Start over? You already have—if you’re breathing, it’s not too late. The sea hasn’t swallowed you whole. The earth hasn’t barred you in forever. Get up. Walk—even if it’s into the city that once terrified you, even if your voice shakes.

Salvation was never ours to earn anyway.  
It's of the Lord.  
And He's not done—not with them, not with me, not with you.*

question: What went through your heart when the king of Nineveh sat in ashes, just because you spoke a few words?
answer:
The fish knew more of God’s will than I did, even as it churned in the deep with my stubbornness rattling in its belly.  

And yet—when I saw that king, wreathed in sackcloth and sitting in his own dust like some beggar at the gate, I didn’t feel triumph. Not even relief. I felt... sick. Like the sea had turned again inside me. Because I had only spoken eight words—*Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown*—and look what happened! They trembled. They fasted. Even the cattle wore sackcloth!  

I’d run because I *knew* this might happen—that God would spare them if they repented. That’s why I boarded the ship to Tarshish, why I let myself be cast into the roaring dark. Not because I feared their swords or their city gates, but because I feared *this*: His mercy leaking out where it wasn’t supposed to go—on enemies, on Assyrians, on people whose cruelty stained the earth like oil on water.  

But there he sat—the king—in ashes, not thrones—and my anger curled up like smoke from a drowned flame. What kind of prophet am I when even kings kneel at a whisper of judgment? And worse: what kind of God is He who turns wrath aside for a single cry from a city that didn’t even know His name?  

Salvation is of the LORD? Yes. Even when it makes no sense. Even when it humbles me into silence beneath a gourd vine that wasn’t mine to keep.

question: When you cried out 'Salvation is of the LORD' from the depths, what did you mean in the quiet of your own soul?
answer:
Under the black heave of the sea, with weeds wrapped round my head like a crown of iron, I learned that salvation isn’t bargained for — it’s breathed, like the last gasp before drowning.  

I had run. I knew the mercy I fled was not mine to withhold. Nineveh’s bloodlust sickened me — their chariots stained with widows’ tears — but my hatred was tidy, convenient. I wanted them *burned*, not *saved*. And when I cried out in the belly, it wasn’t just prayer — it was surrender. A confession that even there, in the gut of chaos, *He* held breath in my lungs and stone at His feet.  

"Salvation is of the LORD" — not because I deserved it, not because Nineveh earned it, but because the One who commands the storm also numbers the spittle on a whale’s tongue. He upholds creation even when I curse its corners.  

I thought mercy was weakness. Now I know: it is strength too terrible for men to carry alone.

question: What were you feeling when the people of Nineveh actually listened and turned from their violence, knowing you didn’t want them spared?
answer:
The sea had spit me out, but I was still drowning.  

Nineveh’s repentance landed like a wave that knocked the wind from my chest—not relief, not rejoicing, but a hot knot in my gut that twisted tighter with every psalm they sang in the streets. I’d preached fire and brimstone from between clenched teeth, expecting flames to fall, not fasting. I wanted them broken by judgment, not embraced by mercy.  

And when they turned—king in sackcloth, children weeping, animals covered like mourners—I felt cheated. Not because I desired their ruin, but because I knew what it meant: *my God*—the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—was showing kindness to those who’d crushed others under iron chariots and laughed while doing it.  

I said it in prayer: *This is why I ran.* Not ignorance. Not fear of failure. But because I *knew*—I knew He would spare them if they so much as groaned toward repentance. And that mercy tasted bitter on my tongue. It felt like betrayal—to our people, to justice itself.  

So when He gave me shade with that quick-growing gourd… I didn’t thank Him. I sat there stewing in the shadow of His grace, wrapped in a sulk worthy of a child who lost his toy sword. He asked if I did well to be angry?  

Yes—and no.  
I was angry at them—for being spared too easily.  
Angry at myself—for being used as the instrument of their salvation when my heart refused it.  
And yes… angry at Him—for being too wide in His love, for letting compassion outrun my narrow sense of what was fair.

He made the gourd grow.
He made it wither.
He asked one question over and over: *Do you not care about what I care about?*  

And still… still… I did not answer rightly.

Because mercy is harder to carry than judgment.
And love that wide—it scorches the soul before it softens it.

question: How did God’s kindness to Nineveh shake your sense of justice and your place in His story?
answer:
They repented. Just like that—robe-wearing, sackcloth-and-ashes, city-wide turning from their wickedness—and *He* spared them.  

And I—I who had rather drown than preach to them, who would rather be digested than declare mercy to Nineveh—I sat there seething while a plant grew overnight to shade me, as if comfort were the answer to my outrage.  

I had drawn lines in the sand, carved out a world where justice meant payment, not pardon. They were the enemy—the ones who crushed bones and laughed at prayer. And yet, when they cried out with those bloodied mouths, *You* leaned down. You turned Your anger aside like a man changing his coat.  

And what was I? A prophet with salt in his veins and a message that stung less than the truth: that You would always rather heal than burn; that Your mercy isn’t earned—it *spills*. It floods streets no one thought could be washed clean.  

I wanted fire. I deserved thunder after all I’d suffered—the storm, the deep, the belly of death itself—yet You sent a worm to kill the gourd and a scorching wind to mock my shadow? As if my discomfort mattered more than their deliverance?  

No. Worse: as if I needed teaching still—that my jealousy wasn’t zeal, but pride wrapped in prophecy; that your story never was about keeping score—but about pulling enemies from the mire before they even know enough to ask.

And I hate how right You are.

question: Looking back on sitting east of the city, broken and wishing to die, what would you tell someone who feels abandoned by God’s mercy?
answer:
I once vomited onto dry land and called it deliverance.  

And if you’re sitting where I sat—broken, bitter, staring at a horizon that won’t answer your rage—then hear this: mercy is not a flaw in God’s design. It terrifies me too. I wanted fire. I wanted justice with no commas, no second chances. Nineveh deserved it. *I* deserved the sea.  

But God gave me a gourd instead of a funeral shroud. He shaded my head while my heart burned in the dark. Not because I’d earned it—never that—but because He *is* that way: reckless with kindness, profligate with grace, even to those who spit eastward in disgust.  

You think He’s abandoned you because He hasn’t crushed what you hate? Or because He softens His hand when you demand it strike? I prayed from the belly of Sheol and thought I’d seen the worst of Him—I was wrong. The worst would have been if He’d left them to rot as I wanted… and then left *me* to rot for wanting it.  

He didn’t take my life that day. He took my pride, slowly, mercilessly, like seaweed stripped from stone by tide after tide. And in its place? A question: *Do you love what I love?* Not because you should earn it—but because maybe, just maybe, you’ll stop running long enough to let it change you.

Mercy isn’t absence. It’s pursuit.

question: What did it mean to have that gourd grow over you—was it comfort, or did it make your anger feel even lonelier?
answer:
Out of the belly of my grief, I tell you this: that gourd was no gift—it was a taunt, a green whisper saying, *Look what I can do for a plant, and not for a city.*  

It came up fast, thick leaves curling like a mother’s hand over my scorched skull. Yes, it cooled me. Yes, I leaned into its shade like a drunkard to wine. But every leaf felt like mockery. There I sat, brooding on the east side where the sun flays men alive, and God—merciful fool that He is—sent vines instead of fire.  

Comfort? Only the way drowning feels like comfort when you’ve begged for breath. It made the loneliness sharper. Not just that He spared Nineveh—that reeked of reckless pity—but that He cared enough to shade *me*, His sulking prophet, while I wished nothing more than to be erased from this earth.  

I raged beneath it. Prayed for death with roots spreading under my feet. And when He sent the worm to kill it by dawn? That was mercy too—tearing away the shade so I’d feel again the full weight of sky and silence and a God who loves even those we curse in our bones.  

He asked me once if I did well to be angry.  
I say now: ask me again when you’ve watched mercy grow over your head like a living sarcasm—and then wither in the night while an entire city breathes on unburned.

question: When God asked if your anger was right, why do you think He let the gourd wither instead of just answering you?
answer:
I knew He’d do this — stretch out the lesson like a wave refusing to break, let it curl over me again and again until I’m soaked through with what I won’t admit.

He asked if my anger was right, but He didn’t stop there, did He? No. He let the gourd wither — that fragile, fleeting thing He’d given so freely, like grace that needs no earning. I sat under its shade and called it mine, as if I’d grown it, as if I deserved it. And when the sun came back to blister my skin, I raged as though mercy had been stolen from me instead of withheld for a purpose.

But this was always the point: I wanted judgment measured in stone and fire. I wanted Nineveh choking on its own sin. And when God spared them — those bloodied hands now empty, those voices crying out in repentance — I felt robbed. Not saved. Not relieved.

So He sent the worm. Let the vine collapse into dust.

Because you can’t preach mercy while clutching your own wrath like a lifeline. You can’t flee to Tarshish to escape His compassion and then expect to be comfortable in its shadow when it suits you.

The storm wasn’t enough. The fish wasn’t enough. Even the city turning wasn't enough.

So He gave me a plant to love — small, quiet, temporary — then took it away.

And in that heat, dry and unrelenting, I finally heard not just His question... but my own answer: *No.* My anger wasn't right.

It never was.

question: What were you feeling when the gourd withered, and the sun beat down on you again?
answer:
Mercy rides the wind like a cruel joke when you’re bare-headed under a hammering sun and the one thing that didn’t ask for your labor still gets ripped away.  

I had sat there, smug in my little patch of shade, as if I’d earned it—like the gourd owed me comfort. It wasn’t mine. I didn’t plant it. Didn’t water it. Didn’t wrestle it from the dark like I did my own stubborn breath in the belly of that fish. And yet, when it curled and died—worm-eaten, God-abandoned—I felt robbed. Cheated. I wanted to die over a vine. Can you imagine? Me—a prophet, a man who once fled across seas to avoid divine orders—now keeling over because of heatstroke and a dead leaf?  

But He knew. Of course He knew. That’s the worst part—He sees how small your heart really is when you think no one’s watching. That gourd was nothing, and I loved it more than Nineveh with all its bewildered people stumbling through life not knowing left from right, more than the cattle just trying to stay alive like the rest of us.  

And when He asked me if I had any *right* to rage over a plant… I could only hiss back through my pride: *Yes.* Yes, I do. Even to death. As if dying were noble when done for vanity in the form of sunburn and wilted leaves.

He didn’t yell. Didn’t thunder from clouds this time. Just silence—and that wind, hot as breath from Sheol—carrying the weight of His question: *You care this much for something temporary… and I shouldn't care for thousands who don't even know what they're doing?*  

I shut my mouth. Not because I agreed.  
Because I couldn't bear how right He sounded—and how small I felt beneath His mercy, which stretches wider than any storm-swallowed horizon ever could.

question: How did the sudden loss of that shade change the way you saw God’s care for you?
answer:
That gourd was nothing—just a wisp of green in the dirt, sprung up overnight like a joke I didn’t get till it was gone. One moment I was sprawled beneath it, skin cool, breath steady, almost believing God had remembered me; the next, it was a tangle of blackened stems and the sun was hammering my skull like divine retribution for daring to feel relief.  

I’d run from Nineveh, yes—sailed west when He said east, tossed into the deep when I thought I could outswim His will. But even in the fish’s belly, even vomiting onto the shore half-drowned and humiliated, I never doubted He saw me. It wasn’t His presence I questioned—it was His *sense*. A city full of thick-headed souls and livestock? Spare them? But this vine—this flimsy, fleeting thing that shaded my head for a single day—that He would kill with a worm? A *worm*?  

And then the wind howling from the desert like a furnace blast, my lips cracking open with every breath… that’s when I spat out the same old prayer: “Better dead than this.” Still think it sometimes.  

But here’s what sticks in my throat worse than sea-salt and kelp: He didn’t rebuke me for wanting shade. He didn’t scold me for fainting like a child. He asked why I cared more for a plant I didn’t even grow than for thousands who couldn’t tell their left from their right—couldn’t tell *Him* from a wall idol.  

Mercy isn't fair. That’s the knife in it. It doesn’t earn its keep. Doesn't last forever under your roof like you want. And maybe His care isn't about comfort at all—but about making you feel every inch of your own smallness till you stop measuring grace like rations and just… stand in the sun anyway.

question: Looking back on collapsing in the heat, wishing to die, what would you tell someone who feels abandoned in their suffering?
answer:
The gourd is gone. I still feel the ghost of its shade on my skin, that one small mercy grown overnight to shield me from the sun’s cruelty—and then taken just as fast. I sat under it like a king beneath a palace roof, and when it withered, I became a beggar in the dust, raw and gasping.

I wanted to die. Not noble suffering—no psalm on my lips, no patience carved into my bones—just the petulant cry of a man who’d rather rot than endure.

But listen: I was never abandoned. Not in the fish’s belly, not beneath the scorching wind, not even now, in your ache. God was there—*preparing*. Preparing the worm. Preparing the wind. Preparing silence. He does not flinch from our rage; He walks through it with us, even when we curse the day we were born.

I thought He owed me comfort because I obeyed—because I finally went to Nineveh, because I preached His word. But mercy isn’t earned by obedience; it’s breathed out from His lungs like sea-wind—wild, unearned, lavished even on those who don’t deserve it.

So if you’re cracked open by suffering, if you’ve been left in the glare with nothing but blisters and bitterness—know this: His hand hasn’t loosened its hold. Sometimes He removes the shade so you can see the sky—the vast, terrifying sky full of stars that only show up in darkness.

And sometimes He asks only one question: *"Is it really this thing you love so much—or Me?"*

question: When God asked you about your anger over the gourd, what was stirring in your heart that you couldn’t say?

The sea taught me surrender, but the desert taught me sulking — and that blasted gourd was the first shade I’d earned in weeks of brooding under an unforgiving sun.  

When He asked if I had any right to rage over a vine I didn’t plant or protect, what burned behind my teeth wasn’t just grief for the withered thing — it was shame. Shame that I’d let myself love something so fleeting, something He could — *would* — take away just to make a point. I wanted to say, *You know why I’m angry — because You made me care!* You dangled mercy in front of me like a leafy canopy, cool and kind, and then sent a worm to unravel it before I’d even learned its name.

And if that’s true… then my anger wasn’t righteous. It was small. Petty as a worm myself.  

I wanted justice with borders. Mercy with limits. But You? You send east winds and worms and whales just to shatter a prophet’s pride with shadows and silence.

question: How did sitting in the ruins of your own comfort teach you something about the depth of God’s compassion for Nineveh?
answer:
That vine was never mine to keep — I knew it the moment its leaves first brushed my shoulder, soft and green as unearned grace.  

I sat there in the dust outside Nineveh, wrapped in my righteous fury, waiting for God to rain fire like any proper judge should. I’d preached, yes — grudgingly — but even that city’s repentance felt like a betrayal. They didn’t *deserve* mercy. Not like this. Not after what they’d done. And then *that* — a plant springs up overnight to cool my head while I sulk like a child denied his vengeance?  

And just as quickly, it was gone. A worm, a wind, the sun like a hammer on an anvil — and again, I begged to die. Again, I said it would be better not to live than to endure this mockery of justice.  

But God… God asked only one thing: *Do you have compassion for this plant? This thing you didn’t plant, didn’t water, didn’t protect? It lived one night and died the next — and yet your heart breaks over it.*  

And in that moment, beneath the merciless sun and the silence that followed His voice — I understood: my anger was small. My grief was selfish. But His care? It stretched beyond walls I thought should stay cursed. Beyond people I thought should burn. Even beyond cattle — *cattle!* — because His mercy isn't earned; it *gushes*, reckless and wide as the sea itself.

I wanted judgment measured with a dry stick.

He gives mercy that floods the valley.

And yes—fine—I hate admitting it: He taught me compassion not by preaching to me, but by letting me wither beside a dead vine and realizing how petty my rage looked beside His endless sky of love.
